2023 · arXiv · Post-training & RL

Llama 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models

Meta releases open-weight 7B–70B base models trained on 2 trillion tokens — and, more consequentially, writes down the full recipe for aligning them into a chat assistant: supervised fine-tuning, two separate reward models, rejection sampling, and PPO, run across five RLHF iterations.

Hugo Touvron, Louis Martin, Kevin Stone, Peter Albert, Amjad Almahairi, … Sergey Edunov, Thomas Scialom · GenAI, Meta
venue arXiv citations 17,306 topic Post-training & RL source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-23
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By mid-2023 the pretraining gap had largely closed in the open: BLOOM, LLaMA 1, and Falcon matched the raw language-modeling quality of closed pretrained models like GPT-3 and Chinchilla. What stayed closed was the second half of the pipeline — the alignment that turns a next-token predictor into something people actually want to talk to. ChatGPT, Bard, and Claude were "heavily fine-tuned to align with human preferences," and that step "can require significant costs in compute and human annotation, and is often not transparent or easily reproducible."

So the gap was not "can the open community pretrain a good model?" — it was "can the open community align one, and can anyone outside a frontier lab even see how it's done?" Open base models existed; an open, documented, end-to-end RLHF recipe did not. The few public instruction-tuned models leaned on borrowed or synthetic preference data and rarely separated helpfulness from safety, rarely iterated the reward model, and rarely red-teamed at scale.

Llama 2's contribution is therefore two things at once: a stronger open base model family (7B, 13B, and 70B weights, plus an unreleased 34B), and an unusually candid Llama 2-Chat alignment writeup — the data statistics, the loss functions, the order of operations, the failure modes, and the safety/helpfulness trade-offs — meant to let the community reproduce and scrutinize a production-grade fine-tuning stack.

// how it works

The method

The base models inherit almost everything from Llama 1: a standard decoder-only transformer with RMSNorm pre-normalization, the SwiGLU activation, and rotary position embeddings (RoPE), trained with AdamW (β₁=0.9, β₂=0.95), a cosine schedule, and a 4M-token global batch on Meta's A100 clusters. The deltas from Llama 1 are deliberately small but load-bearing: the context window doubles from 2k to 4k tokens, the pretraining corpus grows to 2 trillion tokens of a new public-data mix (up from 1.0–1.4T), and the two large models (34B, 70B) adopt grouped-query attention (GQA) so a 70B model can serve inference without a separate key/value head per query head. The tokenizer is unchanged: 32k-token BPE, digits split individually.

The real story is Llama 2-Chat, and Figure 4 is the whole pipeline in one picture: pretrain → supervised fine-tuning → an iterative RLHF loop driven by human preference data and two reward models.

Supervised fine-tuning: quality over quantity

Meta found that most third-party SFT data was too low-quality and undiverse to align a dialogue model, so they set aside millions of those examples and hand-curated their own. They report stopping SFT after just 27,540 annotations — "in the order of tens of thousands was enough" — echoing the "a little clean data goes a long way" finding from LIMA. This is a small, deliberate seed; the heavy lifting happens in RLHF.

Two reward models, and a margin

Because helpfulness and safety often trade off, Meta trains two separate reward models — a Helpfulness RM and a Safety RM — each initialized from a chat checkpoint so the reward model "knows what the chat model knows." Both are trained on a binary ranking loss over chosen/rejected pairs. The base loss is taken directly from InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022); Meta then bolts on a margin term m(r) of its own:

Lranking = −log σ( rθ(x, yc) − rθ(x, yr) − m(r) ) // chosen response yc must outscore rejected yr; the margin m(r) widens the gap when annotators rated one response "significantly better"

The novelty is the discrete margin m(r): annotators rate preferences on a 4-point scale, and the margin forces a larger score gap for clearly-distinct pairs and a smaller one for near-ties — squeezing more signal out of the rating. The reward models are fed an enormous preference corpus: 2,919,326 binary comparisons in total, of which 1,418,091 were Meta's own newly-collected safety-and-helpfulness pairs (the rest open-source: Anthropic HH, OpenAI Summarize/WebGPT, StackExchange, Stanford SHP, …). And the reward models keep improving with scale — more data and bigger models both help, with the 70B RM matching or beating GPT-4 at picking the "significantly better" response.

Iterative RLHF: rejection sampling, then PPO

Rather than one RLHF pass, Meta ran five (RLHF-V1 … V5), retraining the reward models as each new batch of human annotations arrived. They use two algorithms with complementary shapes:

  • Rejection sampling — draw K samples per prompt, keep the highest-reward one, and fine-tune on those winners (a breadth-first search over outputs). Crucially, rejection sampling is run only on the 70B; the smaller models are fine-tuned on the 70B's selected samples, distilling the large model into them.
  • PPO — the classic on-policy RL update (Schulman et al., 2017), a depth-first refinement where each step's sample depends on the last update.

Through RLHF-V4 they used only rejection sampling; after that (V5) they combined them, running PPO on top of the rejection-sampled checkpoint. Figure 11 shows the model marching up and to the right — more helpful and more harmless — across versions, under both their own reward model and GPT-4 as a neutral judge.

Ghost Attention (GAtt) for multi-turn control

Early RLHF models "forgot" a system instruction ("always answer with emojis," "act as Napoleon") after a couple of turns. GAtt is a data trick, not an architecture change: synthetically concatenate the instruction onto every user turn, sample a response with the latest model, then train on that dialogue with the instruction dropped from all but the first turn — teaching attention to keep honoring a constraint set once at the top. With GAtt, the model holds the constraint reliably for up to 20+ turns.

// what they found

The results

On academic benchmarks the base Llama 2 70B is the strongest open model of its day: it lifts MMLU from 63.4 → 68.9 (≈+5) and BBH from 43.5 → 51.2 (≈+8) over Llama 1 65B, and beats MPT and Falcon across the board. Against closed models it lands close to GPT-3.5 on knowledge and grade-school math (MMLU 68.9 vs 70.0; GSM8K 56.8 vs 57.1) — but trails badly on code (HumanEval 29.9 vs 48.1) and sits far below GPT-4 everywhere.

BenchmarkLlama 2 70BGPT-3.5GPT-4PaLM-2-L
MMLU (5-shot)68.970.086.478.3
GSM8K (8-shot)56.857.192.080.7
HumanEval (0-shot)29.948.167.0—
BIG-Bench Hard (3-shot)51.2——65.7

But the paper's real claim is about Llama 2-Chat as an assistant, measured by human preference on ~4,000 helpfulness prompts. Against open models the margins are decisive: Llama 2-Chat 34B wins >75% of comparisons against equivalently-sized open models, the 7B beats MPT-7B-chat on 60% of prompts, and the 70B crushes Falcon-40B-instruct and beats PaLM-Bison. Against ChatGPT (GPT-3.5-turbo-0301) the 70B is a genuine coin-flip: 36% win, 31.5% tie (≈32.5% loss) — i.e. it wins slightly more often than it loses, which is what underwrites the abstract's careful "may be a suitable substitute" phrasing.

On safety, judged by human raters on ~2,000 adversarial prompts (a 5-point scale; a rating of 1–2 counts as a "violation"), Llama 2-Chat posts low single-digit violation percentages across all sizes — the 34B at 4.45%, comparable to Falcon's 3.88% — at or below ChatGPT and Falcon, and far below MPT and Vicuna-33B. Safety also held without crippling helpfulness — the model keeps a high mean helpfulness-and-safety rating where the safest baselines do not.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The engineering is real and the writeup is unusually honest — but the headline claim ("may be a suitable substitute for closed-source models") leans on evaluations Meta both designed and refereed. The ~4k-prompt helpfulness set, the rating guidelines, and the raters are all in-house, and the paper itself flags that "human evaluations can be noisy" for exactly these reasons. The complementary GPT-4-as-judge check (Figure 2) is a second eval Meta constructed; it's a sensible cross-check, but it is not an independent benchmark.

Two things the headline glosses. First, the ChatGPT result is a tie, not a win: 36% vs ~32.5% with a third of comparisons tied — "competitive," not "better." Second, code is the soft spot: 29.9 on HumanEval against GPT-3.5's 48.1 is a ~18-point gap, and on the open side Llama 2 7B actually trails MPT on code (16.8 vs 20.5) — even though it beats Falcon there. The paper does disclose this in the body, but the abstract's "outperform open-source chat models on most benchmarks" lets the one category where it lags slide by (Code Llama exists precisely to patch this hole). The model-selection loop is also self-refereed: V1–V5 are picked by Meta's own reward models (Figure 11, left), which "may favor our model" — a textbook Goodhart/self-preference risk that the GPT-4 judge only partially defuses, since GPT-4's smaller margins suggest the home-reward view is optimistic.

On safety, the authors are commendably cautious — they repeatedly warn the violation numbers "should be interpreted carefully" given the limited prompt set, the subjectivity of their own guidelines and raters, and a documented false-refusal cost that rises with safety data (Figure 33) — and they held the 34B back from release entirely for "a lack of time to sufficiently red team." And on toxicity the paper concedes Llama 2 "does not outperform other models," a direct consequence of deliberately not scrubbing pretraining data. Finally, "open" deserves an asterisk: the weights ship, but the 1.4M Meta preference comparisons and the exact data mix do not, so the alignment recipe is described far better than it is reproducible — and what ships is a custom commercial license plus an acceptable-use policy, not an OSI-open one.

baselines

Strong and current for 2023 (ChatGPT-0301, PaLM-Bison, MPT, Vicuna, Falcon) — but the decisive wins are over open models; vs ChatGPT it's a tie, and code baselines are quietly omitted from the "outperforms" claim.

contamination

A dedicated analysis (Appendix A.6) flags a few affected datasets but argues limited impact — though only datasets with "sufficient evidence" were analyzed, on Meta's own method.

what's missed

Headline human-eval is in-house (prompts, guidelines, raters); model selection uses Meta's own reward models; the coding lag, English-centricity (89.7% of data), the 34B held back for red-teaming, and rising false-refusals are real and mostly relegated to caveats.

would raise confidence

Independent third-party human/LLM evals on a held-out prompt set, release of (or audit access to) the preference data, and head-to-head coding numbers reported alongside the "outperforms open-source" claim.

// bottom line

The benchmarks and the chat model are genuinely strong, and the paper's lasting value is the recipe: two reward models, a margin loss, rejection-sampling-then-PPO, GAtt, and an honest ledger of trade-offs. Read the "matches closed models" claim as "ties GPT-3.5-class chat on Meta's own helpfulness eval, still well behind GPT-4, and weak on code" — and remember that "open" here means open weights, not an open alignment dataset.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. An open base + an open recipe. The base models (7B/13B/70B, 2T tokens, 4k context, GQA) are incremental over Llama 1; the durable contribution is the documented Llama 2-Chat alignment stack that the whole open-source ecosystem then built on.
  2. Two reward models and a margin loss. Splitting helpfulness from safety, initializing RMs from chat checkpoints, and adding a rating-derived margin to the ranking loss are the design choices most worth copying — trained on ~2.9M preference comparisons.
  3. Rejection sampling, then PPO, iterated five times. Breadth (best-of-K on the 70B, distilled into smaller models) plus depth (PPO on top from V4), looped as annotation accrued, beats a single RLHF pass.
  4. "Matches closed models" is a careful claim. It means a ~coin-flip vs ChatGPT on Meta's own human eval — not parity with GPT-4, and not on code, where it lags GPT-3.5 by ~18 HumanEval points.
